coredata.dc:title: Evaluating the efficacy of specific aptamers for post-translational modified hemoglobin A by developing a lateral flow assay based on fluorescent carbon dot signals and smartphone-based read-out with quantification application software
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tajadini, Mohamadhasan
Namin, Seyed Alireza Mesbah
Moshaii, Ahmad
Rasaee, Mohammad Javad
coredata.dc:description: Post-translational modifications (PTMs) change the properties of proteins in ways that often change the binding efficiency of antibodies to the altered proteins. Since developing potent antibodies to neutralize the effects of PTMs has been challenging, developing simpler antibody development methods or alternatives such as aptamers is increasingly being considered. In this study, dual-target specific aptamers for total hemoglobin A (HbA), including hemoglobins A1c (HbA1c) and A0 (HbA0), were developed by a modified SELEX method to neutralize the effects of PTMs, and their in-field performance was evaluated using a quantitative lateral flow assay in a sandwich format based on the aptamers, fluorescent carbon dots synthesized from lemon juice, a smartphone camera reading-unit, and a dedicated MATLAB-based application software. We generated five high-affinity aptamers with dissociation constants in the low nanomolar range from 30 to 150 nM. Carbon dots were synthesized using lemon juice by hydrothermal method with a size of about 10–15 nm and excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 480 nm, respectively. The resulting measurement achieved analytical catachrestic of the linear range of 1–28 g/dl, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.33 g/dl, and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1.00 g/dl using standard curve analysis. The spike and recovery method achieved analytical accuracy with a recovery rate of 91 % to 108 %. In-field agreement of the method to a reference method was analyzed by Bland–Altman analysis with the bias and limit of agreement (LOA) of 0.1 g/dl and −0.65 to 0.45 g/dl with the predicted 95 % confidence intervals, respectively. Considering the results of this study, using the modified SELEX method, the obtained aptamers, in addition to being specific, neutralized the effect of PTMs, allowing the development of a measurement method with appropriate analytical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Post-translational modifications
Dual-target aptamers
Hemoglobin A
Quantitative lateral flow assay
Fluorescent carbon dots
Smartphone read-out
MATLAB-based application

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PCR, DNA purification, single-stranding and assessment
Synthesis of carbon dot as a fluorescent material
Architecture and fabrication of lateral flow assay strip
Analyzing the different combinations of aptamer sandwiches
Image analysis of different combinations of aptamer sandwiches
Method performance, precision, reliability and agreement analysis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Tarbiat Modares University [80866]. This research has been approved by the Tarbiat Modares University ethics committee with the IR ethics code.MODARES.REC.1398.158. Also, no human or animal sampling was done in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a and b show the side and top views of the read-out unit, respectively. a: The smartphone is placed on top of the cube chamber so that its camera faces the cassette on the other side. Two LEDs are placed inside the chamber on both camera sides towards the cassette. The LEDs' energy source and the on and off switch are shown in a structure. b: This image shows how the camera and LEDs are positioned relative to the test area window on the cassette. Since the camera is on the smartphone's right side, the cassette's entrance and location are also designed accordingly. c: Top view of the test cassette. The dashed large and small circles refer to the sample inlet funnel and hole placed directly above the sample pad. The dashed rectangle shows the cassette window corresponding to the test line region and directly on the nitrocellulose membrane.

Electropherograms were obtained from capillary electrophoresis with a column temperature of 25 °C and voltage of 25 kV. a, b, and c represent the peaks obtained from Human Serum Albumin (HSA), hemoglobin, and ssDNA library electrophoresis, respectively. Since the buffer and electrophoresis conditions were selected for the SELEX, the method could not separate HbA1c and HbA0.

a Aptamers 1 to 5 Binding curve for HbA1c and HbA0 targets with the non-linear regression analysis results. The dashed lines represent the 95 % confidence band of the best-fit curve. b: Dissociation Constants of the Aptamers for HbA1c and HbA0 targets. The results are expressed as Mean ± SD. Calculations and graphs were made using GraphPad Prism software (version 9.4.1), which used non-linear regression parameters and a one-site-specific binding equation.

Bland–Altman plot comparing capillary electrophoresis (Reference method) and our method (Field method) HbA measurements (n = 40). Thex-axis is the average of the two measurements. They-axis is the bias that is the difference between the two measurements. Bias: −0.1 g/dl and LOA: −0.65 to 0.45 g/dl.
Sequences of the five aptamers. Predicted the putative Quadruplex forming G-Rich sequences is listed in bold and underlined.

row.entry:
ATCATCCAGCTTATTCAATTGCTGGTTTCCC GG GGGC GG CGTA GG GGCCC GG CGCCCGAAAG
TGGATGGAGATAGTAAGTGTGCAGGAGA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The recoveries of spiked HbA samples.

head.ce:title: Evaluating the efficacy of specific aptamers for post-translational modified hemoglobin A by developing a lateral flow assay based on fluorescent carbon dot signals and smartphone-based read-out with quantification application software

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran 1411713116, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran 1411713116, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran 1411713116, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran 1411713116, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Post-translational modifications (PTMs) change the properties of proteins in ways that often change the binding efficiency of antibodies to the altered proteins. Since developing potent antibodies to neutralize the effects of PTMs has been challenging, developing simpler antibody development methods or alternatives such as aptamers is increasingly being considered. In this study, dual-target specific aptamers for total hemoglobin A (HbA), including hemoglobins A1c (HbA1c) and A0 (HbA0), were developed by a modified SELEX method to neutralize the effects of PTMs, and their in-field performance was evaluated using a quantitative lateral flow assay in a sandwich format based on the aptamers, fluorescent carbon dots synthesized from lemon juice, a smartphone camera reading-unit, and a dedicated MATLAB-based application software. We generated five high-affinity aptamers with dissociation constants in the low nanomolar range from 30 to 150 nM. Carbon dots were synthesized using lemon juice by hydrothermal method with a size of about 10–15 nm and excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 480 nm, respectively. The resulting measurement achieved analytical catachrestic of the linear range of 1–28 g/dl, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.33 g/dl, and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1.00 g/dl using standard curve analysis. The spike and recovery method achieved analytical accuracy with a recovery rate of 91 % to 108 %. In-field agreement of the method to a reference method was analyzed by Bland–Altman analysis with the bias and limit of agreement (LOA) of 0.1 g/dl and −0.65 to 0.45 g/dl with the predicted 95 % confidence intervals, respectively. Considering the results of this study, using the modified SELEX method, the obtained aptamers, in addition to being specific, neutralized the effect of PTMs, allowing the development of a measurement method with appropriate analytical properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Post-translational modifications (PTMs) alter the physical and chemical properties of proteins, such as structure, activity, aggregation, shelf life, and immunogenicity[1], which usually change the binding efficiency of antibodies toward altered proteins[1,2]. So, there has been a high demand for anti-PTM antibodies, but developing potent antibodies to PTMs has been challenging[3]. If the antibody needs to recognize whole protein (both intact and PTM-affected), this situation becomes more complicated, mainly rooted in the complexity of the developing process of antibodies[4]. So, simpler methods of antibody development or alternative options instead of antibodies are increasingly considered.

Aptamers are short strands of single-strand DNA or RNA that could mimic the function and are considered their most important alternatives for antibodies[5,6]. The promising potential and high capacity for modification and improvement of this discovery encouraged many scientists, and many studies have been done in this regard [4,7–10]. This trend is expected to continue with the elimination of existing problems such as inefficient SELEX method[11], Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) bias in amplification[12], loss of epitope[13], sub-optimal SELEX condition[11], unwanted cross-reactivity[14], orientation on surfaces[15], un-wanted interaction with surfaces[11].

Hemoglobin A (HbA)is the most common adult form ofhemoglobin[16]. A fraction of HbA can undergo covalent modification through a spontaneous chemical reaction due to adding a free amine group from the protein to the carbonyl of reducing sugars. This process occurs through an irreversible non-enzymatic reaction called glycation, leading to the formation of sub-fraction of glycated HbA such as HbA1c[17], which is defined as the most stable adduct of this reaction and can reach more than 60 % intact protein[18]. Glycation leads to one of the most significant chemical and physical changes in hemoglobin, altering many of its normal functions and characteristics. The severity of these changes is so great that the altered form has been given a new name.

The unique properties of HbA1c as a PTM and the hypothesis of the need for suitable antibody alternatives that could bypass the effects of PTMs and allow recognition of the entire protein motivated us to investigate the possibility of developing aptamers with such properties. However, the efficacy of the resulting products needed to be tested in real-world conditions. In this study, we attempted to develop specific dual-target aptamers with high affinity for HbA1c and HbA0 using periodic-SELEX, a modified selex method. Such aptamers can be attached to the target protein without being affected by PTMs, and they can design robust measurement methods. Therefore, a quantitative lateral flow assay based on a sandwich structure with developed aptamers, fluorescent carbon dots prepared from lemon juice for the signal, a measurement device based on a smartphone read-out unit and specific software was designed and developed to investigate the practical efficiency of the aptamer, and its analytical efficiency was measured to confirm the efficiency of the aptamers.

HbA1c was purchased from BBI Solutions (Crumlin, UK). HbA0, human serum albumin, TRIS, KH2PO4, Glycine, Ethanol, and CaCl2, ethylenediamine, dichloromethane, streptavidin, glutaraldehyde, sodium bicarbonate, sodium borohydride, and AmiconUltra-15 centrifugalfilter were purchased from Merck-Millipore (Darmstadt, Germany). TGK buffer consisting of 0.312 g/l Tris, 14.4 g/l glycine, and 0.68 g/l KH2PO4 was used as separation and sample buffer. The single-strand DNA library consisted of 49 random bases flanked by two Constant primer regions (5′-ATCATCCAGCTTATTACAATT (49 random bases) AGATAGTAAGTGTGCAGGAGA −3′ and forward, 5′-phosphorylated reverse primers and 5′- biotinylated aptamers were purchased from TIB MOLBIOL (Berlin, Germany). PCR master mix consisting of Taq polymerase enzyme, dNTPs, and buffer was purchased from Sinaclon (Tehran, Iran). Intact pTZ19R vector, Eco321 enzyme, lambda exonuclease enzyme, and T4 DNA ligase enzyme were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (MA, USA). FavorPrep GEL/PCR purification mini kit was purchased from Favorgen (Ping Tung, Taiwan). RBC lysis buffer was purchased from MAN (Tehran, Iran). Filter tips, tubes, and 0.22um syringe filter were purchased from Extragene (Taichung, Taiwan). All paper materials used in lateral flow assay were purchased from Cytiva (DE, USA).

In this study, we used an Agilent 7100 capillary electrophoresis device (CA, USA) equipped with a UV/Vis detector and a bare fused silica (uncoated) capillary column with an inner diameter of 50 um, a total length of 40 cm and a distance of the detector window from the inlet of 31.5 cm. All thermal conditions and PCR tests were applied in a Veriti thermal cycler (MA, USA). An Agilent Biotek synergy H1 multi-mode microplate reader (CA, USA) was used for optical measurements and determination of fluorescence polarization. A Philips EM 300 (Amsterdam, Netherlands) was used for Transmission Electron Microscopy.

For capillary electrophoresis, the preconditioning steps included three flashing steps of two minutes of water, five minutes of 1 M NaOH, and three minutes of TGK buffer. Two tandem 0.22 um syringe filters prepared all solutions and liquids. The sample was injected for five seconds at a pressure of 100 mbar. The column temperature was set at 25 °C, and all tests were performed with a voltage of 25 kV for 5 min. The dedicated ChemStation software did the related analyses.

To determine the fraction collection window, ssDNA library, Hb, and human serum albumin were subjected to electrophoresis separately, and the passing times through the detector were recorded at 260 nm, 410 nm, and 280 nm, respectively. The time for the targets to reach the column outlet was obtained using the tout = (LT) (tdet)/LD formula, where tout is the time for the targets to reach the column outlet, LT is the total length of the column, tdet is the time for the targets passing detector, and LD is the length from inlet to the detector. For normal-SELEX fraction collection window was from the beginning of electrophoresis to the tout Hb + 1/2(tout ssDNA + tout Hb), and for counter-SELEX, it was tout from the starting of ssDNA peak to the end of the electrophoresis. Fig. S1 in Supplementary material shows the strategies for determining the set of windows.

The periodic-SELEX procedure was carried out in nine rounds, so ssDNA samples were subjected to CE with HbA0 and HbA1c alternately. Nine rounds of selection were done for HbA1c and HbA0 in 4 rounds each. In the first, third, sixth, and eighth rounds, the selection was made for HbA1c, and in the second, fourth, seventh, and ninth rounds for HbA0, the counter selection was made in the fifth round for human serum albumin.

Before performing the CE, the ssDNA sample in TGK buffer was kept at 70 °C for ten minutes and then cooled to room temperature. Then, the target protein was added and allowed for 20 min until the reaction reached equilibrium. Before performing SELEX rounds,

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCR, DNA purification, single-stranding and assessment
ce:section.ce:para: After performing electrophoresis in each round, the isolated sample in the outlet vial was subjected to PCR using specific primers. The PCR conditions included a 94 °C step for 5 min and 20 cycles, and each cycle consisted of three 30-second steps of melting at 94 °C, annealing at 55 °C, and amplification at 72 °C. After confirmation by gel electrophoresis, the product was purified using a PCR purification kit to remove PCR reaction materials. Single-stranding was done using lambda exonuclease enzyme for 120 min at 37 °C, and the single-stranded product was purified the same way as the PCR product. dsDNA and ssDNA concentration and purity were measured at 260 nm and 260/280, respectively. The final product was used for the next round.

The TA Cloning method was used to clone the PCR product from the last round of SELEX. First, the Eco321 enzyme cut the pTZ19R vector, and T-overhang was added to the created blunt end by Taq polymerase enzyme and dTTP nucleotide. Then, the PCR products were ligated into the prepared vector using T4DNA Ligase enzyme at 16 °C overnight and transformed into TOP10 chemically competent E.coli cells using heat shock for 90 s at 42 °C. Then, the cells were suspended in an LB-broth medium and shaken at 37 °C for 60 min at 200 rpm in a rotary shaking incubator. The obtained suspension was transferred to an LB-agar plate containing five mg/l of tetracycline antibiotic and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The obtained single colonies were subjected to colony-PCR using M13 primers, and the same conditions were used as the PCR performed in SELEX to confirm the transformation and get the sample for further sequencing. After confirmation of transformation by agarose gel electrophoresis, the samples obtained from the colony-PCR were subjected to PCR with the primers and condition used in SELEX and, after single-stranding with exonuclease lambda enzyme, were used to determine the dissociation constant.

Dissociation constant measurement was done for two purposes: First, during the SELEX process, to determine the sufficient number of selection rounds (bulk dissociation constant) and monitor the trend of changes in the affinity of the ssDNA pool toward targets, and second, to determine the affinity of the aptamer obtained from the last selection round. Three sets, each consisting of nine samples 0–2 μM of HbA1c and HbA0, and 0–250 nM of human serum albumin and 25 nM of heated and cooled ssDNA pool which prepared in TGK buffer, separately incubated at room temperature for 20 min to reach equilibrium for bulk dissociation constant and aptamer dissociation constant. Then, samples were subjected to CE with the conditions stated for the selection steps, and the decrease in the height of the aptamer peak (bound-aptamer) was noted. The fraction of bound-aptamer was obtained using the H0-H/H0 formula, where H0 is the height of the unbound aptamer peak in the absence of the target, and H is the height of the unbound-aptamer peak. The fraction of bound-Aptamer plotted against the concentrations of the target. Calculations and graphs were made using GraphPad Prism software (version 9.4.1), which used non-linear regression parameters and a one-site-specific binding equation. In the SELEX procedure, no significant increase in the bulk dissociation constant was considered a sufficient number of rounds.

The aptamers of five clones for which affinity tests were performed were sequenced, and their sequences were subjected to bioinformatic structural investigations. The obtained sequences were analyzed by web-based mFold software to predict the structure and determine the free energy (ΔG). In addition, web-based QGRS Mapper software was used to determine the putative Quadruplex forming G-Rich Sequences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot as a fluorescent material
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dot was synthesized hydrothermal, and lemon juices were used ascarbon sources. 50 ml of filtered fresh lemon juice and 1 ml of ethylenediamine as a nitrogen-rich source were mixed in a hydrothermal reactor and kept at 180 °C for six h. The dark paste product was cooled to room temperature, dissolved in 10 mL of pure water, and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to separate insolubles. Then, dichloromethane was added to the solution and centrifuged for another round at 4000 rpm for 20 min to remove unreacted organic materials. The separated upper layer was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min to remove larger-sized particles, and a brown-yellowish supernatant was finally obtained.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis characterized the prepared carbon dots for the morphology and size distribution using a Philips EM 300 Transmission Electron Microscope at 200 kV. An Agilent Biotek synergy H1 microplate reader was used for optical measurements and determination of fluorescence polarization.

In order to confirm the binding of carbon dots with streptavidin, fluorescence polarization studies were performed, and the fluorescence polarization value was compared for conjugated and non-conjugated carbon dots. The excitation and emission wavelengths were 380 nm and 530 nm, respectively.
The higher magnitude of Fluorescence polarization for the post-conjugation step compared to unconjugated carbon dots confirmed the binding of carbon dots to streptavidin.

Since this assay was a sandwich format, it required a primary aptamer attached to streptavidin (capture) and a secondary aptamer (reporter) attached to the signalling material, a fluorescent carbon dot.

For primary aptamer, 200 μL of 200 μM of each 5′-biotinylated aptamer was added dropwise at a molar ratio of 2:1 to ice-cold stirring streptavidin (0.1 mg/ml) for 1 h. The result solution was directly filtered to a final volume of 200 μl with an Amicon Ultra-15 centrifugalfilter (Molecular weight cut-off of50 kDa) to be used as a primary aptamer.

For secondary aptamers, glutaraldehyde-mediated conjugation was performed to bioconjugate the carbon dot with streptavidin-biotinylated aptamers. 450 μl of 1 M sodium bicarbonate buffer was added to 1 ml (400 mg/ml) of carbon dots. Then 50 µl of 10 % (w/v) glutaraldehyde were added to this solution dropwise with stirring for 2 h.

After that, 200 μl of streptavidin (1 mg/ml) was added dropwise to the reaction mixture for 2 h on ice. Then, 2 mL of freshly prepared sodium borohydride (20 mg/mL) was added dropwise to the reaction mixture on ice and incubated overnight at 4 °C. The final solution was filtered three times by an AmiconUltra-15 centrifugalfilter (Molecular weight cut-off of50 kDa) with TGK as a wash buffer for the final volume of 200 μl. Finally, 200 μL of 200 μM of each 5′-biotinylated aptamers were added dropwise to 20 μl ice-cold carbon dot bioconjugated streptavidin for 1 h and was filtered thrice by an AmiconUltra-15 centrifugalfilter (Molecular weight cut-off of 50 kDa) with TGK as a wash buffer for the final volume of 200 μl. All solutions were kept at 4 °C for future use.

The read-out unit consisted of a central cube-shaped chamber made of 5 mm thick black foam board sheets, on which the Xiaomi Redmi Note 8 smartphone with the macro-camera (48-megapixel, pixel size: 0.8 μm, f/1.8 aperture) was placed at a suitable focal distance of 10 cm from the cassette of the test strip in front of it. The double UV light source, a carbon-dot exciter, was also installed on both sides of the camera toward the strip so that it would not directly radiate to the camera. The energy source was a rechargeable 600 mAh battery placed in the circuit using an on/off switch. Fig. 1 shows the Read-out unit's general and simplified schematic view in two lateral and a top view. A top view of the strip cassette is also shown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Architecture and fabrication of lateral flow assay strip
ce:section.ce:para: The strip architecture, designed with the size of 80 mm × 6 mm, includes a 2-ply 20 mm long GF/DVA glass fiber sample pad, a 10 mm long GF/F glass microfiber pad for filtering cell debris and unwanted particles, a 20 mm long FF80HP nitrocellulose membrane for the test lines containing 1.5 nmol immobilized primary streptavidin conjugated aptamer which printed by a reagent dispenser and a 2-ply 36 mm CF4 cotton paper absorbent, respectively, all of which were fixed on a backing card with an overlap of 2 mm. Fig. S2 in Supplementary material schematically shows the designed test structure. The top part of the Fig. S2 shows the lateral view, and the bottom part shows the top view.

An application was developed to quantify the results of the photos taken. The code of this application was written in the app design environment of MATLAB version 2020b, and after editing the written codes, the resulting application was used. Five images were taken from each strip test and analyzed by the application, and the average results and CVs were recorded.

For analyzing the aptamer combinations, 10 μl of 8 g/dl HbA and for analysis of the method reliability, 10 μl of spiked 0 to 32 g/dl HbA samples with 2 g/dl intervals were added to 125 µl of sample buffer containing 0.5 % Tween 20, 8 μl of secondary carbon dots conjugated aptamer and 70 μl of 2× TGK buffer. After 5 min, the resulting solution was poured into the cassette funnel. The cassette is placed inside its Read-out unit, and a photo is taken after 5 min of pouring the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyzing the different combinations of aptamer sandwiches
ce:section.ce:para: Each of the five aptamers was used as the primary (capture) aptamer, with itself and the other four aptamers as secondary (reporter) aptamers to create a sandwich assay. As a result, 25 combinations of aptamer pairs were investigated. Such a design allowed us to investigate the possibility of creating a sandwich with different combinations of aptamers and assessing the possible inhibitory interactions related to their binding.

Seventeen samples from 0 to 32 g/dL HbA with 2 g/dl intervals were analyzed in quintuplicate. After quantification, CVs were calculated to check the precision. A standard curve was drawn using Microsoft Excel software (CA, US) for the method's reliability parameters. Change in R2 values was used to determine the linear range. By using Excel regression analysis, the standard deviation (SD) of the response on blank (σ) and the slope(s) of the calibration curve were obtained, and the Limit Of Detection (LOD) and Limit Of Quantification (LOQ) were calculated using formulas 3.3 * σ/s and 10* σ/s, respectively.

Spike and recovery analysis was performed in triplicate to validate and assess the accuracy of the analysis. Plasma samples separated from blood with previously determined hemoglobin concentration were used, and three samples of low (4 g/dl), medium (12 g/dl) and high (24 g/dl) within the linear range of the standard curve were prepared using HbA solution. An equal volume of RBC lysed buffer was added to the neat sample, and the recovery was calculated using the recovery formula:[19]
Recovery % = Measured concentration Spiked sample − Measured concentration Neat sample/Theorical concentration Spiked *100.

To compare the developed method to a reference method, 40 whole blood samples were analyzed after obtaining informed consent from the donors. Ten μL of EDTA-treated whole blood sample was added to 130 μL of lysis solution, shaken vigorously, and after 5 min, 65 μL of 3× sample buffer was added to the solution. After 5 min, the resulting mixture was analyzed using a lateral flow assay test with aptamers 4 and 3 as primary and secondary aptamers, respectively. Results obtained by the read-out unit were compared with those of the pre-determined HbA levels obtained by the capillary electrophoresis using the Sebia Capillary 2 Flex device. A difference analysis (Bland-Altman plot) was performed using DATAtab online Statistics Calculator software (https://datatab.net) to assess bias and limits of agreement (LOA) (bias ± 1.96 SD) between capillary electrophoresis (Reference method) and our method (Field method) [20,21].

Fig. 2 showed the results of capillary electrophoresis of HSA, HbA1c, and HbA0 and Single Strand Deoxyribonucleic Acid (ssDNA), Peaks with times of 1.96 (1.85–2.53), 1.436 (1.28–1.78), and 3.023 (2.94–3.08) minutes passing the detector, respectively. Based on these results, there was enough time between the peaks of the library and the target proteins. According to the times obtained and the formula for calculating targets to reach the column outlet presented in the methods section, times from the beginning to 2.81 min were used to collect the Hb-ssDNA complex fraction for SELEX rounds and from 3.7 min to the end of collection time (5 min) for counter-SELEX. The ssDNA library's final concentration was 200 μM in the first round, and due to the limitations of the PCR method and purification methods, it was reduced to about ten µM in the subsequent rounds.

We isolated specific dual-target aptamers with the ability to bind specifically to more than one target with a modified method called Periodic-SELEX. Usually, in the studies done in the field of aptamer development, ligands with an affinity to specific targets have been isolated, and cross-reactive structures have been undesirable[14,22]. Trying to isolate aptamers that can bind to multiple targets will not produce the desired results if the method is not controlled. In this study, we successfully isolated aptamers with high affinity to bind HbA1c and A0 targets by modifying the SELEX method in a controlled manner. Alternative treatment of the ssDNA pool with two targets of HbA1c and A0 allowed us to evolve aptamers over rounds of SELEX for common parts of the two targets.

Our study was conducted in 9 rounds (4 rounds for each target), although the number of rounds was less in the other studies that used the CE method [23–26]. On the one hand, it should be considered that in the mentioned studies, the aptamers were isolated only for just one specific target. On the other hand, it should be kept in mind that the evolution of the SELEX process and the binding of aptamers in the common areas between two targets have been carried out in more rounds to complete the SELEX process, and the obtained aptamers have undergone further evolution.

In studies where the SELEX process has been performed using the CE method, counter-SELEX is usually not used because of some consideration toward its performance and Necessity[27]. Our study showed that using counter-SELEX decreased the affinity of the ssDNA pool for albumin as an undesirable target to unmeasurable levels, which led to the isolation of specific aptamers for Hb targets and did not interfere with the separation of aptamers with high specificity.

As mentioned before, the isolation method used in this study to separate aptamers was CE, which, besides having suitable analytical features, was ideal for SELEX. In CE, in addition to benefiting from a high level of sensitivity, automation, low volume of sample consumption [28–30], and a significant reduction in the number of SELEX rounds and time, there is no need to use fixation targets on a surface such as a column or beads[31]. Due to the availability of all the target surfaces, ssDNA in the environment can freely interact with the targets. Also, there is no need to do the washing steps in normal SELEX, and the probability of analytical errors and kinetic biases is reduced [23].

Bulk dissociation constant (Kd) analysis showed that after the third round for both targets, there was no significant increase (Cumulative round 6 for HbA1c and round 7 for HbA0). So, the selection process ended in the fourth round for both Targets. While the amount of Kd for both HbA targets showed a decreasing trend (increasing affinity), this trend was increasing for albumin, and finally, after performing the counter-SELEX in the fifth round, the amount of Kd could not be measurable in the following rounds. Fig. S3 in Supplementary material shows the process of changes in Kd during the SELEX rounds.

Aptamer dissociation constant was done for five random clones. Fig. 3 shows the result of the measurement of dissociation constants in the low nanomolar range from 30 to 150 nanomolar for the targets. No measurable dissociation constant was obtained for human serum albumin under the same conditions as HbA1c and A0. A decrease in the affinity of aptamers was observed with the increase of SELEX cycles for some clones, which was observed in a similar study by Yang and Bowser [32]. They proposed that this deterioration in the binding efficiency is probably due to the PCR amplification bias for sequences with lower guanine content. As the selection continues and the abundance of binding sequences increases, PCR efficiency may play a more critical role, impacting the selection than the CE separation. They also raised the possibility of a flaw in the affinity measurement method. However, this phenomenon was not observed in our study.

The sequences of aptamers and structures with the lowest ΔG predicted by mFold software are shown in Table 1 and Fig. S4 in Supplementary material, respectively[33]. The function of an aptamer depends on its ability to fold into the most stable possible structure and maintain a binding-competent conformation[34]. Thus, the ΔG for a given DNA sequence is an essential determinant of aptamer stability, affecting the affinity and specificity of the aptamer for its target[35]. QGRS Mapper software also predicted the putative Quadruplex forming G-Rich sequences for the fifth aptamer, which is listed in bold and underlined form in the fifth aptamer sequence in Table 1. G-quadruplex structures are secondary structures that couple a scaffold to different motifs, which can act in target recognition. Generalizing the free energy affinity study results and secondary structures, although indicating a higher affinity for the structure with the probability of forming a G-Quadruplex, the affinity values are not significantly different. In particular, the results obtained for secondary structures are from computer algorithms and web-based simulations, and the translation of these results into actual test conditions may differ. Therefore, we believe that the best way to determine the efficiency of an aptamer is through its performance results in the field and not only the results from in silico studies.

The structure of the read-out unit is shown in Fig. S5 in Supplementary material. Inside the camera unit was a battery on which a recharging circuit was placed, with the USB Type-C port facing outwards. Also, two 380 nm LEDs were placed on both sides of the smartphone camera slot towards the cassette and the strip test location (8a). In order to reduce the temperature, increase the lifespan, and achieve uniform performance, the LEDs were mounted on heat sinks. The cassette entrance slot was also located at the bottom and right side of the unit (8b). The smartphone was also placed on the unit so that its camera was perpendicular to the area of the strip test line (8c).

The quantitative measurement package was a read-out unit based on an LED excitation source and fixed-point imaging, a 48-megapixel camera smartphone. In addition to the possibility of building a small unit, such a structure was cheap, easy to update, and tuneable to appropriate wavelengths. Also, the use of cheap foam board material to make the unit and ease of construction made the product even cheaper.

Using TEM analysis, the size and morphology of carbon dots were determined. Based on the images obtained from the transmission electron microscope shown in Fig. S6 in Supplementary material, the particles were well-dispersed and uniformly spherical nanoparticles with an average particle size of about 10–15 nm. As for optical properties, the excitation spectrum peaked in the violet area at 380 nm, which led to the emission peak in the blue-cyan area at 480 nm. Fig. S7 in Supplementary material shows absorbance, excitation, emission curves, and photos of carbon dots solution under daylight and 380 nm LED with brown-yellowish and bright cyan colours, respectively. In this study, we chose a carbon dot as a fluorescent label. In recent years, carbon dots as carbon-based nanomaterials by virtue of their unique structure and fascinating biological, electronic, and optical properties such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, excellent photostability, tunable fluorescence, photoluminescence behaviour have attracted considerable interest in biological sensing[36–38]. Carbon dot has suitable optical properties for use as a label, and bioconjugation can be done easily. Also, the synthesis method and raw materials used made its production very economical [37]. The carbon dots in this study were synthesized using the bottom-up method using lemon juice precursor and through doping strategy using ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source, which has a bright blue-cyan fluorescent at a wavelength of about 580 nm which was due to 380 nm excitation. In this study, the approximate cost of carbon dot synthesis and bioconjugation was about $1 per 200 tests, which was very affordable.

The conjugation of carbon dots was confirmed by the fluorescence polarization value of 182 mP compared to the 41 mP of unconjugated carbon dots. The size of the particles is the reason for the existence of different polarization values. Un-conjugated carbon dots excited by plane-polarized light emit mostly depolarized light because of the faster rotation between excitation and emission. However, the larger streptavidin-conjugated carbon dots rotate more slowly, and the emitted light remains polarized mainly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Image analysis of different combinations of aptamer sandwiches
ce:section.ce:para: The Read-out unit obtained the images of 25 different aptamer combinations. The combination of each aptamer with itself and aptamers 1 and 3, 1 and 5, 2 and 4, and 4 and 5 did not result in a fluorescent signal in the test line. The combination of aptamers 2 and 3 had significantly less intensity than other combinations (P-value <0.05), and while the combination of primary and secondary aptamers and vice versa had almost similar results (P-value >0.05), the combination of aptamers 2 and 5 was significantly different (P-value <0.05). The sample images of aptamer combinations with positive results, their normalized intensity, and the application's user interface are shown in Fig. S8 in Supplementary material.

In order to check the best aptamer combination and the inhibitory effect of aptamers, different aptamer combination sandwiches were investigated. The results showed that the sandwiches of the same aptamer did not lead to the same result. The reason for such an observation is probably due to the specificity of aptamers for single epitopes on the target or spatial interference due to Adjacent epitopes[39]. The last case is probably the reason for the negative result in a sandwich with some aptamer combinations. The reason for observing partial results is probably attributed to partial spatial interference[40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method performance, precision, reliability and agreement analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S9 in Supplementary material shows the results of method precision and reliability analysis. A sudden decrease in R2 value occurred at a concentration of 30 g/dL, which means a linear range up to a concentration of 28 g/dL. The LOD and LOQ were calculated 0.33 g/dl and 1.00 g/dl, respectively, as reliability parameters, according to σ: 0.75 and s:7.51.

A linear range of 4.5 to 25 g/dl, an accuracy of ± 1.75 g/dl, a CV of 2 %, and a sample volume of 20 μl are the minimum recommended values for a quantitative method to measure hemoglobin[41]. The developed test linear range and analytical accuracy were 0 to 28 g/dl and ± 0.476 g/dl, fulfilling the recommended criteria. However, the test CV (5.84 %) did not fulfil the recommendation. Also, LOD and LOQ are six units less than the minimum acceptable hemoglobin level[42]. The noteworthy point in the standard curve is the decrease in intensity at the concentration of 32 g/dl compared to the previous concentration, probably due to the quenching phenomena. When the molecular concentration of fluorophores increases, the fluorescence quantum yield decreases [43]. This phenomenon occurred in our study at a concentration higher than the test's maximum normal hemoglobin level and linear range. The occurrence of the concentration quenching phenomenon is probably the factor limiting the linear range of the test; however, it did not affect the efficiency of our test for the reasons mentioned.

To check the method's accuracy, a recovery test was performed at three levels: low, medium, and high, which showed a recovery of 91 % to 108 %. The results of the test are shown in Table 2 . The results showed recovery within the acceptable recovery rate of 80–120 %[19]. These results indicate no significant interference from the sample matrix that could be responsible for interfering with the measurement of the HbA.

Fig. 4 shows a comparison of HbA measured by a capillary electrophoresis (Reference method) and our method (Field method). Differences between the two measurements are plotted against their averages. This helps to visualize the agreement between the two methods and identify any systematic bias. The bias and LOA between capillary electrophoresis (Reference method) and our method (Field method) were −0.1 and −0.65 to 0.45 g/dl, respectively.

We needed to assess the agreement between the two designed methods (Field method) and the reference method of measurement. Validation of a clinical measurement method is a lengthy and compelling process that requires an acceptable LOA between the two techniques[44]. When the methods being compared are continuous variables, the Bland-Altman analysis is suitable for performing this comparison and provides quantitative criteria for deciding whether or not the new method is acceptable[45]. Considering the low bias and the fact that the results of the two methods differ within one gram, the new method seems to provide a good performance in terms of accuracy compared to the reference method under consideration.

In this study, by designing, fabricating, and assembling a package, we developed a low-cost lateral flow assay with aptamer-based capture and reporter, fluorescent carbon dot signal, smartphone-based imaging, and dedicated application-based analysis with reasonable analytical performance that confirmed the efficacy of isolated specific dual-target aptamers. Considering our study's simplicity, applicability, and results of aptamer evolution, we concluded that the periodic-SELEX method is suitable for obtaining high affinity specific dual-target aptamers. In applications of biological structures with binding properties such as antibodies and aptamers, obtaining those that can bind to multiple targets with high affinity can be essential. It should be noted that although in this study, in order to show the effectiveness of the method, the aptamer was used in the form of a quantitative identification test, such structures can be used in the selective and specific removal of unwanted molecules or substances, neutralizing toxins, and as multi-target binding molecules in other application.

This work was supported by the Tarbiat Modares University [80866]. This research has been approved by the Tarbiat Modares University ethics committee with the IR ethics code.MODARES.REC.1398.158. Also, no human or animal sampling was done in this study.

Mohamadhasan Tajadini: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Seyed Alireza Mesbah Namin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Ahmad Moshaii: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Javad Rasaee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank our Isfahan Applied Physiology Research Center (APRC) colleagues for greatly assisting the research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112396.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
